original_hypothesis.title: SignalService.sendSignal allows arbitrary users to write unbounded signal slots, enabling state bloat / long-term storage DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: sendSignal(bytes32) is permissionless and uses raw `sstore(slot, value)` into a slot derived from (chainid, msg.sender, signal). Attackers can spam many unique signals, permanently increasing SignalService state size and raising long-term node storage burden. While per-tx gas cost limits throughput, this is a practical grief vector on L2s with cheaper gas and no protocol-level cleanup.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: SignalService is core infrastructure and unpausable. Persistent storage bloat can degrade state size, indexing, and potentially affect future upgrades/migrations. Since sendSignal has no rate-limits or fees beyond gas, spam is straightforward.

proof_of_signal.pattern: permissionless arbitrary sstore into attacker-chosen unique slots

preconditions: Attacker can pay gas to call sendSignal repeatedly
Attacker uses many unique `_signal` values to force new storage slots

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: permanent state growth in core contract
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No throttling, no deletion, no payable fee mechanism

analysis_questions: Is SignalService intended to be permissionless for app signals (likely yes)?
What are the acceptable bounds for state growth on target L2s?
Are there mitigations at protocol layer (e.g., separate per-app SignalService instances)?
validation_requirements: Estimate cost per new slot (20k gas) and realistic spam budgets on target chain
Assess operational impact on nodes/indexers for sustained spam
Check if upgrades can introduce pruning or migration

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker runs a bot that calls sendSignal with random signals, creating millions of storage slots over time and increasing chain state size for all participants.
prerequisites: No admin pausing (SignalService is intentionally unpausable)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect ecosystem cost (state bloat) rather than immediate fund loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Hard; requires protocol-level pruning or migration

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No stress tests for storage growth

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Cost per new slot, throughput on L2, long-term projections
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor growth of SignalSent events and storage usage

validation_result.impact_summary: The hypothesis describes a general blockchain state growth property, not a code defect. sendSignal works exactly as designed.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that SignalService.sendSignal allows unbounded storage writes constituting a DoS vector. While the observation that sendSignal is permissionless and writes to unique storage slots is factually correct, this is the intended design of the SignalService contract. Any application or user is supposed to be able to send signals - this is core cross-chain messaging infrastructure. The slot derivation keccak256(abi.encodePacked('SIGNAL', chainId, msg.sender, signal)) namespaces signals per sender, so callers can only write to their own signal namespace. The gas cost per new SSTORE (20,000 gas for cold slot) IS the rate-limiting mechanism, which is the standard Ethereum model for all storage-writing operations. This same 'vulnerability' applies to any ERC20 transfer (creates new balance slots), any ERC721 mint, any mapping write in any contract. There is no code defect here - no missing check, no incorrect formula, no logic error, no standards violation. The function performs exactly its intended purpose correctly.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: SignalService.sol sendSignal() correctly calls _sendSignal(msg.sender, _signal, _signal). The _sendSignal function has validSender and nonZeroValue modifiers, computes a deterministic slot, performs sstore, and emits an event. All of this is correct behavior for a signal service. The lack of additional rate-limiting is a design choice, not a defect.

calling_sequence: sendSignal(_signal) external - any caller allowed by design
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: There is no vulnerable code path. The function works as designed. Each call costs gas (20,000+ for new slot), which is the standard rate-limiting mechanism for all blockchain storage operations.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show SignalService is deployed as a shared contract with permissionless sendSignal by design. The authorize function is only for syncChainData, not sendSignal.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The described 'attack' - calling sendSignal with many unique signals to create storage slots - is functionally identical to any other blockchain spam: sending many small ERC20 transfers, creating many contracts, etc. On L2s with cheaper gas, ALL storage operations are cheaper, not just sendSignal. This is a general L2 design consideration, not a SignalService-specific vulnerability. At 20,000 gas per new slot, even at 0.01 gwei gas price, each slot costs 0.0000002 ETH. Creating 1 million slots would cost ~0.2 ETH in gas alone, producing only 32MB of state growth - trivial for modern nodes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No code defect exists. The described behavior (permissionless storage writes costing gas) is the fundamental model of all Ethereum smart contracts. This is a design concern about state growth that applies equally to the entire EVM ecosystem, not a specific bug in SignalService.
recommended_mitigations: This is a design-level consideration, not a code fix. If desired, protocol-level state expiry or storage rent could address general state growth.
For defense-in-depth, consider monitoring SignalSent events for unusual spam patterns at the operational level.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy SignalService, call sendSignal with many unique values, measure gas cost per call (~23,000-26,000 gas including overhead), confirm standard SSTORE gas pricing applies. Compare state growth rate to any other contract with public storage writes.
uncertainties: L2 gas pricing models vary and could make spam cheaper than on L1, but this applies to ALL L2 operations equally
additional_testing_needed: Benchmark actual gas costs on target L2 for sendSignal vs other common operations to confirm no anomalous cheapness
